category: B6 — Keep commandments / obey God's moral law
interpretive_notes: The most comprehensive single ethical principle in Paul — extends beyond specific commands to all of life. Key text for the soli Deo gloria tradition.
literary_context: 1 Corinthians 10; the idol-food discussion; summary principle for all disputed matters.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Whether you eat or drink, or whatever you do, do all to the glory of God.

interpretive_notes: Key principle: the earth belongs to God, so meat is not inherently defiled. Only abstain if someone explicitly identifies it as idol meat and a weaker conscience would be damaged.
literary_context: 1 Cor 10:23-33 — Paul's practical resolution of the idol-food question; permits eating marketplace meat and dinner-party food without inquiry.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Eat meat sold in markets without asking if it was sacrificed to idols; eat whatever is offered at an unbeliever's dinner.

interpretive_notes: Qualifies the freedom of 10:25-27: uninformed eating is permitted, but once informed, eating becomes a statement of indifference that harms the weaker person's conscience.
literary_context: 1 Cor 10:23-33 — practical guidance on idol food in social settings; when specifically told food was sacrificed, refraining is required for conscience's sake.
q2_notes: Explicit conditional command with clear logic — yet inapplicable in most modern contexts (no idol sacrifices). Treated as culturally irrelevant through implicit Purpose–Form reasoning.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
subtheme: Don't Eat When Informed It Was Sacrificed
summary: If told the food was sacrificed to idols, do not eat it — for conscience's sake.

interpretive_notes: The escalation in 1 Cor 10: eating marketplace meat is fine (vv.25-27); eating at a pagan idol-temple feast is not.
literary_context: 1 Cor 10:1-22 — Paul escalates the idol-food argument to a warning about actual participation in idol feasts at pagan temples.
ot_antecedent: Exod 32; Num 25 (examples Paul cites in context)
q2_notes: The prohibition against idolatry is universally affirmed and consistently applied across all traditions.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Flee idolatry.

category: A6 — Pursue economic equality / oppose exploitation
interpretive_notes: Economic inequality at the Eucharist is treated as a desecration; shaming the poor is equated with despising the church of God.
literary_context: Lord's Supper abuse (1 Cor 11:17–34); some eat lavishly while the poor go hungry at the communal meal; Paul rebukes sharply.
speaker: Paul
subtheme: Shaming the Poor at the Lord's Supper
summary: Some go hungry while others feast — you humiliate those who have nothing at the Lord's table.

interpretive_notes: The appeal to 'nature' here is culturally contingent for many interpreters; hair length norms vary across cultures and eras.
literary_context: 1 Cor 11:2-16 head-covering discussion; Paul appeals to 'nature' as corroborating evidence.
q2_notes: CRITICAL CASE: Paul appeals to "nature itself" (C1=P — as strong a grounding as creation order). Yet virtually no evangelical church enforces a rule against men having long hair. The same "nature" argument is not treated as binding here but analogous arguments elsewhere are. The criterion is applied selectively.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Nature teaches that long hair is shameful for men but glorious for women.

interpretive_notes: The 'unworthy manner' in context was the class-based abuse of the Supper (rich eating while poor went hungry). The self-examination command is the remedy Paul prescribes.
literary_context: 1 Cor 11:17-34 — Paul's corrective following the institution narrative; Corinthians were treating the Supper in a way that shamed the poor.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Each person to examine themselves before taking communion; eating unworthily brings judgment.

interpretive_notes: Debated: whether 'head covering' is cultural (Roman/Jewish custom) or universally normative. Paul grounds it both in creation order AND custom, creating interpretive complexity.
literary_context: 1 Cor 11:2-16 — discussion of head coverings in corporate worship; Paul appeals to creation order, nature, and Corinthian practice.
q2_notes: CRITICAL CASE: Paul appeals to "glory" and "angels" as grounding (C1=P). C4=N: the same chapter assumes women CAN pray and prophesy (11:5) — if 14:34-35 (women silent) is universal, this contradicts 11:5. Most evangelicals treat head coverings as cultural while treating women-not-teaching as binding — but both are in Pauline epistles, both address gender in corporate worship.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Women praying or prophesying in worship must cover their heads or else should shave their heads (as a disgrace).

interpretive_notes: The most widely observed NT liturgical command; debated in terms of frequency ('as often as'), mode, and theological significance (symbol vs. real presence).
literary_context: 1 Cor 11:17-34 — Paul corrects the Corinthian Lord's Supper abuses; quotes the institution narrative received from the Lord.
q2_notes: Dominical command; universally observed.
speaker: Jesus (quoted by Paul)
summary: Institution of the Lord's Supper: 'Do this in remembrance of me' — eat the bread, drink the cup.

category: A3 — Give to the needy / almsgiving
interpretive_notes: Generosity without love is empty; love is the motive that makes mercy meaningful.
literary_context: Love chapter (1 Cor 13); Paul places love as the indispensable foundation beneath all gifts and acts, including total self-giving.
speaker: Paul
summary: If I give all I have to the poor but have not love, I gain nothing.

interpretive_notes: A summary command; notably does not abolish tongues. The positive command to prophecy is often underweighted in cessationist readings.
literary_context: Conclusion of 1 Cor 14 — summary of Paul's instructions on spiritual gifts in worship.
q2_notes: C4=N: This command appears in the same chapter as 14:34-35. Churches that enforce women's silence (from this chapter) while forbidding tongues are selectively applying two commands from the same passage.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Eagerly desire prophecy; do not forbid tongues; do everything orderly.

interpretive_notes: The underlying principle is intelligibility and edification; Paul does not abolish tongues/prophecy but orders them.
literary_context: 1 Cor 14 — Paul regulates the exercise of spiritual gifts in corporate worship; climax of the tongues/prophecy discussion.
q2_notes: The orderly-worship principle is universally applied.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Detailed instructions: tongues only with interpreter, prophets in turns (2-3), others judge; orderly and edifying.

interpretive_notes: Significant tension with 1 Cor 11:5 where women praying/prophesying is regulated but not prohibited. Some scholars treat vv.34-35 as a scribal gloss; others as restricting a specific kind of speech (disruptive questioning). Widely debated.
literary_context: 1 Cor 14:26-40 instructions on orderly worship including prophecy and tongues; the silence command comes at the end of this section.
q2_notes: CRITICAL CASE: C4=N: 1 Cor 11:5 assumes women pray and prophesy in the assembly. These commands cannot both be universally binding without contradiction. The silence command likely addresses a specific disruptive practice (chattering, questioning during service). If treated as universal, it prohibits female singing, female testimony, and female prophecy — which most complementarian churches permit.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Women commanded to be silent in church assemblies and ask questions of husbands at home.

category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
gk_keywords: hedraios (steadfast); ametakinētos (immovable); perisseuō (abound); ergon kyriou (work of the Lord); kopos (labor); kenos (vain)
interpretive_notes: The practical payoff of the resurrection argument — if Christ is raised, our work matters. Three imperatives: be steadfast, be immovable, abound.
literary_context: 1 Corinthians 15; closing verse of the resurrection chapter; the ethical conclusion of the resurrection argument.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Be steadfast, immovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord — knowing that in the Lord your labor is not in vain.

category: A2 — Feed the hungry / provide for bodily needs
interpretive_notes: First explicit instruction for regular, systematic financial giving; proportional to prosperity.
literary_context: Practical instructions (1 Cor 16); Paul commands a systematic weekly collection for the Jerusalem saints.
speaker: Paul
summary: Set aside a sum every week — systematic, pre-planned giving for the poor among the saints.

category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
gk_keywords: grēgoreite (be watchful); stēkete (stand firm); andrizomai (act courageously); krataioō (be strong)
interpretive_notes: Four staccato imperatives forming a martial sequence. The verb andrizomai (be courageous/manly) echoes LXX military exhortations (Josh 1:6-9).
literary_context: 1 Corinthians 16; closing exhortations; four rapid imperatives before the love-command (16:14).
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Be watchful, stand firm in the faith, act like men, be strong.

interpretive_notes: This is the biblical basis for weekly, proportional giving; the context is specifically relief for the impoverished Jerusalem church — a mercy/justice act framed as financial practice.
literary_context: 1 Cor 16:1-4 — instructions for the Jerusalem collection; one of Paul's major charitable fundraising projects (cf. 2 Cor 8-9; Rom 15:26).
q2_notes: Systematic proportional giving.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Set aside money systematically each week in proportion to income for the collection for poor believers.

interpretive_notes: One of five NT occurrences; formulaic closing instruction.
literary_context: 1 Cor 16 — closing of the epistle; standard Pauline closing greeting.
q2_notes: Second occurrence. See Rom 16:16.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Greet one another with a holy kiss.

interpretive_notes: Carefully qualified: the separation is only from professing Christians, not unbelievers. The list of sins is broader than the specific incest case.
literary_context: 1 Cor 5 — Paul's instructions on the case of incest in the Corinthian church; the non-association command applies specifically to professing believers.
q2_notes: CRITICAL CASE: Paul lists sexual immorality alongside greed (pleonektēs) and slander. Most evangelical churches pursue discipline for sexual immorality but not for documented greed or slander among members — a selectivity that directly contradicts Paul's list.
q2_typical_application: SELECTIVE (applied to sexual immorality; greedy/slanderers less enforced)
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Do not associate with or even eat with a person who calls himself a brother but lives in ongoing sin.

interpretive_notes: Paul quotes Deuteronomic expulsion formula (exarō) to validate the command; the church's integrity requires active removal, not passive toleration, of the persistently immoral member.
literary_context: 1 Cor 5:9-13 — instructions on church discipline; Paul distinguishes between not associating with immoral outsiders (impossible) and immoral insiders (required).
q2_notes: Applied primarily for sexual immorality; rarely for the other sins in Paul's list (1 Cor 5:11).
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Remove the evil person from among you — church discipline extends to expulsion of the unrepentant immoral member.

gk_keywords: adikoí (unrighteous); basileian theou ou klēronomēsousin (will not inherit the kingdom); planaō (be deceived)
interpretive_notes: [RESEARCHER TO COMPLETE]
literary_context: 1 Corinthians 6 on lawsuits and sexual immorality; follows rebuke of tolerance of sin within the church (5:1-13).
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Do not be deceived — the unrighteous (including the sexually immoral, idolaters, thieves, the greedy, drunkards) will not inherit the kingdom of God. A broad repentance warning encompassing the full scope of moral failure.

interpretive_notes: Paul's rhetorical escalation (will judge the world, judge angels) grounds the prohibition in eschatological dignity. He does not address disputes with unbelievers.
literary_context: 1 Cor 6:1-11 — Paul rebukes believers taking disputes to pagan courts; argues for internal church resolution.
q2_notes: CRITICAL CASE: Explicit command form; addresses a universal situation (disputes between believers); no cultural specificity. C4=N: rarely enforced — evangelical churches routinely allow and participate in civil litigation between members. By any consistent criterion that makes 1 Tim 2:11-12 binding, this command should be equally binding.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Christians should not take disputes against fellow believers before secular courts; settle them within the church.

interpretive_notes: [RESEARCHER TO COMPLETE]
literary_context: 1 Corinthians 6 on lawsuits and sexual immorality; follows argument that the body is not for sexual immorality (6:13-17).
q2_notes: Explicitly grounded in creation theology (body as God's temple). The command itself IS the theological point — no form-purpose separation. Standard application is consistent. MCA is P because the scope of porneia is debated in specific cases.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Flee sexual immorality — every other sin is outside the body, but sexual immorality is a sin against one's own body, which is the temple of the Holy Spirit. Glorify God in your body.

interpretive_notes: Notably mutual: the wife's authority over the husband's body is stated as clearly as vice versa — unusual in ancient household codes.
literary_context: 1 Cor 7 — Paul's extended response to questions about marriage and celibacy; notably mutual in its structure.
q2_notes: This is a mutuality command — neither spouse withholds from the other. Universally applied and internally consistent.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Both spouses commanded to fulfill conjugal duty; neither has sole authority over their own body.

gk_keywords: deomai; aner; eleuthera; monon en Kyriō
interpretive_notes: The 'only in the Lord' qualifier is the basis for evangelical teaching against marrying unbelievers (cf. 2 Cor 6:14 application).
literary_context: 1 Cor 7:39-40 — Paul's instruction on remarriage of widows; conclusion of the chapter on marriage.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: A widow is free to remarry, but only to a believer ('in the Lord').

interpretive_notes: Paul attributes this specifically to 'the Lord' — echoing the dominical sayings on divorce. Limited exception clauses appear in Matthew's version.
literary_context: 1 Cor 7 — Paul distinguishes his own instruction from direct dominical command (cf. Matt 5:32; 19:9).
q2_notes: Universally applied with contextual qualification for abuse/abandonment.
speaker: Paul citing dominical tradition
summary: Wives told not to divorce husbands; if separated, remain unmarried or be reconciled. Husbands must not divorce wives.

interpretive_notes: Paul's primary concern is not the act of eating per se but the effect on weaker believers whose conscience would be defiled. The principle: limit personal freedom for the sake of others.
literary_context: 1 Cor 8 — Paul's extended treatment of food offered to idols; he affirms knowledge that idols are nothing but prioritizes the conscience of weaker believers.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Don't let your freedom regarding idol food become a stumbling block for the weak.

category: B6 — Keep commandments / obey God's moral law
interpretive_notes: Commandment-keeping is the epistemic criterion for knowing God; the claim without the practice is self-deception.
literary_context: Walking in the light (1 John 2:1–11); John identifies commandment-keeping as the test of genuine knowledge of God.
speaker: John
subtheme: Commandment-Keeping as Evidence of Knowing God
summary: We know him if we keep his commandments — to claim knowledge without obedience is to lie.

gk_keywords: mē agapate (do not love); kosmos (world); epithymia sarkos (desire of the flesh); epithymia ophthalmōn (desire of the eyes); alazoneia biou (pride of life)
interpretive_notes: Three categories of worldly desire mirror the temptation narratives (Gen 3:6; Matt 4:1-11). "World" (kosmos) here means the system of values opposed to God, not creation itself.
literary_context: 1 John 2; between the "children, fathers, young men" addresses (2:12-14) and the antichrist warning (2:18ff).
speaker: John (Elder)
summary: Do not love the world or the things in the world — the desire of the flesh, the desire of the eyes, and the pride of life are not from the Father but from the world.

interpretive_notes: The pairing of belief and love as a single entolē is theologically significant: orthodoxy and orthopraxy are inseparable.
literary_context: 1 John 3:19-24 — assurance before God and the double commandment; faith and love are combined as a single command.
speaker: John
subtheme: Faith and Love as Double Command
summary: His commandment is to believe in Jesus Christ and to love one another — these two together constitute 'the commandment'.

category: A2 — Feed the hungry / provide for bodily needs
interpretive_notes: Closing the heart against a needy brother is the test case for the absence of God's love; love is in deed, not word.
literary_context: Abiding in love (1 John 3:11–18); John argues that love must be concrete; uses a poverty scenario to test whether God's love indwells.
speaker: John
summary: If you have goods and see a brother in need but close your heart — God's love does not abide in you.

interpretive_notes: John roots the love command in the foundational apostolic proclamation, not a new ethical innovation; frames love as identity-constituting.
literary_context: 1 John 3:11-18 — love is the test of being children of God vs. children of the devil; Cain is the counter-example.
speaker: John
summary: Love one another — this is the original message from the beginning.

category: B2 — Believe / have faith in Christ
interpretive_notes: Unique in the NT in framing belief itself as a commandment (entolē); links faith and love as equally commanded.
literary_context: 1 John 3:19-24 — assurance and the double commandment; faith and love are presented as a single divine entolē.
speaker: John
summary: His commandment is to believe in the name of Jesus Christ — belief is not optional but commanded.

interpretive_notes: The indicative (purifies himself) functions as an imperative by definition — this is what hope in Christ entails; purity is both eschatological and present.
literary_context: 1 John 3:1-3 — children of God and the hope of being like Christ; the eschatological hope motivates present purification.
speaker: John
summary: Everyone who hopes in Christ purifies themselves just as he is pure.

category: B6 — Keep commandments / obey God's moral law
interpretive_notes: The connection between commandment-keeping and answered prayer is direct; obedience creates the relational context in which petition is heard.
literary_context: 1 John 3:19-24 — assurance before God; commandment-keeping is both the condition of answered prayer and the content of the double commandment.
speaker: John
summary: Keep his commandments and do what pleases him — then whatever you ask you will receive.

interpretive_notes: Love of others is evidence of regeneration and knowledge of God; its absence is evidence of not knowing God.
literary_context: God is love (1 John 4:7–21); John grounds horizontal love in God's nature and in the sending of Christ.
speaker: John
summary: Let us love one another — love is from God; whoever loves is born of God and knows God.

category: B8 — Guard sound doctrine / contend for the faith
interpretive_notes: The primary NT command for spiritual discernment. The test is christological: does the spirit confess Jesus Christ come in the flesh (4:2-3)?
literary_context: 1 John 4; follows the "abide in him" section (3:24); introduces the criteria for testing: confession that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh.
speaker: John (Elder)
summary: Do not believe every spirit, but test the spirits to see whether they are from God — for many false prophets have gone out into the world.

interpretive_notes: Incarnational confession is the Christological test; a doctrinal boundary that distinguishes truth from falsehood.
literary_context: Test the spirits (1 John 4:1–6); false prophets have gone out; confession of incarnation is the doctrinal test.
speaker: John
summary: Every spirit that confesses Jesus Christ come in the flesh is from God — denial marks the antichrist spirit.

category: B2 — Believe / have faith in Christ
interpretive_notes: Assurance of salvation is a gift to those who believe; certainty is the intended experience of faith.
literary_context: Purpose statement (1 John 5:13–21); John closes the letter by naming its purpose — assurance of eternal life to believers.
speaker: John
subtheme: Belief and Assurance of Eternal Life
summary: I write so that you who believe may know you have eternal life.

gk_keywords: parrēsia (confidence); aiteō (ask); kata to thelēma autou (according to his will); akouō (hear)
interpretive_notes: The condition 'according to his will' is not a restriction but an alignment — prayer that conforms to divine purpose is the mode of confident petition.
literary_context: 1 John 5:13-15 — assurance of life and answered prayer; prayer according to God's will is the condition of confident petition.
speaker: John
subtheme: Confident Prayer According to God's Will
summary: If we ask anything according to his will, he hears us — and we have whatever we have asked.

category: B6 — Keep commandments / obey God's moral law
interpretive_notes: The denial of burden (bareiai) answers an implicit objection; love-as-obedience is not heavy compliance but relational fidelity.
literary_context: 1 John 5:1-5 — born of God overcomes the world; love for God is defined as commandment-keeping.
speaker: John
summary: Love for God is defined as keeping his commandments — and they are not a burden.

interpretive_notes: ektenos implies sustained, strenuous love; this is not incidental but the culmination of spiritual formation described in vv.13-21.
literary_context: 1 Pet 1:13-25 — call to holiness; love is the natural outcome of purification through obedience to the truth.
speaker: Peter
summary: Love one another earnestly from a pure heart — purification by the truth enables genuine love.

interpretive_notes: Direct quotation of Lev 19:2; holiness pervades all conduct; grounded in divine character.
literary_context: Living as exiles (1 Pet 1:13–21); Peter grounds holiness in God's own character and quotes Lev 11:44.
speaker: Peter
subtheme: Be Holy as God is Holy
summary: Be holy in all your conduct — as the one who called you is holy.

category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
gk_keywords: anazōsamenoi (gird up); dianoias (minds); nēphontes (sober-minded); elpisate (set your hope); apokalypsis (revelation)
interpretive_notes: Three imperatives for the waiting community: prepare, be sober, hope. "Gird up the loins of your mind" echoes the Passover readiness (Exod 12:11).
literary_context: 1 Peter 1; follows the praise section (1:3-12); opens the ethical exhortation section of the letter.
speaker: Peter (apostolic letter)
summary: Prepare your minds for action, be sober-minded, and set your hope fully on the grace that will be brought to you at the revelation of Jesus Christ.

gk_keywords: apechomai (abstain); sarkikos epithymia (fleshly desires); kalē anastrophē (honorable conduct); paroikos (sojourner); parepidēmos (exile)
interpretive_notes: Exile identity grounds the ethical demand. Honorable conduct is missional — it leads outsiders to glorify God "on the day of visitation."
literary_context: 1 Peter 2; the elect-exile identity (2:9-10) leads to the ethical imperative; opens the social-conduct section.
speaker: Peter (apostolic letter)
summary: Abstain from the passions of the flesh — as sojourners and exiles — and keep your conduct among the Gentiles honorable, so that they may glorify God.

interpretive_notes: Uses Christ's passion as the paradigm for servant endurance; theologically loaded but also used historically to counsel patient suffering of injustice.
literary_context: 1 Peter 2:11-3:12 household code section; follows call to live as sojourners and precedes wives/husbands code.
q2_notes: CRITICAL CASE: The specific form here is NOT separable from the purpose (C2=N): the command explicitly uses Christ's suffering under unjust authority as the model. If the form is culturally relative, so is the Christological argument. If the Christological argument is binding, so is the specific submission to unjust masters.
speaker: Peter (apostolic letter)
summary: Servants commanded to submit even to harsh masters, following Christ's example of unjust suffering.

interpretive_notes: Four-fold summary of the Christian's social posture: universal respect, communal love, divine reverence, civic honor. The fourfold structure is strikingly balanced.
literary_context: 1 Peter 2:13-17; the governing-authority section; v. 17 summarizes the social ethic in four commands.
q2_notes: "Honor everyone" and "love the brotherhood" are universally affirmed. "Fear God" is universal. But "honor the emperor" is applied with significant qualification — evangelicals affirm civil respect in principle but resist when authority contradicts conscience.
q2_typical_application: SELECTIVELY APPLIED (first three clauses affirmed; "honor the emperor" qualified)
speaker: Peter (apostolic letter)
summary: Honor everyone. Love the brotherhood. Fear God. Honor the emperor.

gk_keywords: apotithēmi (put away); kakia (malice); dolos (deceit); hypokrisis (hypocrisy); phthonos (envy); katalalia (slander); logikon gala (spiritual milk); epipotheō (long for)
interpretive_notes: Put-off/desire-for structure parallels Paul's put-off/put-on ethics. The five vices are all community-destructive; the milk is community-building.
literary_context: 1 Peter 2; opens with moral stripping (put away) and transitions to spiritual nourishment (desire milk); precedes the "living stones" passage.
q2_notes: Grounded in new birth ("like newborn infants"). Vices listed are universal; spiritual growth imperative is straightforward. Aligns directly with mercy commands (putting away malice, slander).
speaker: Peter (apostolic letter)
summary: Put away all malice, deceit, hypocrisy, envy, and slander. Like newborn infants, long for the pure spiritual milk, that by it you may grow up into salvation.

gk_keywords: hypotagēte (submit); ktisis anthrōpinē (human institution/creation); basileus (emperor); hēgemones (governors)
interpretive_notes: The phrase 'for the Lord's sake' (dia ton kyrion) grounds submission in mission and theology, not civic duty alone; submission is a form of proclamation.
literary_context: 1 Pet 2:13-17 — submission to human authorities framed as a witness strategy ('for the Lord's sake'); contextualised in the suffering-servant theology of the letter.
q2_notes: Same selective application as Rom 13:1-7.
speaker: Peter
summary: Submit to every human institution — emperor and governors — for the Lord's sake, as a witness.

interpretive_notes: Mercy disposition as community ethic for believers under social pressure; tenderness is a command, not a suggestion.
literary_context: Living as God's people (1 Pet 3:8–12); community ethics for Christians living as aliens in hostile society.
speaker: Peter
summary: Have unity, sympathy, brotherly love, a tender heart, and a humble mind.

interpretive_notes: Personal witness in response to questions; gentleness and respect qualify the manner; hope is the visible quality that prompts questions.
literary_context: Living as God's people (1 Pet 3:13–17); Peter addresses believers under social pressure; readiness to explain faith is the command.
speaker: Peter
subtheme: Be Ready to Give a Defense
summary: Always be prepared to give a defense for the hope in you — with gentleness and respect.

interpretive_notes: Baptism saves through conscience-appeal not physical washing; grounded in resurrection; carefully qualified statement.
literary_context: Noah's ark and baptism (1 Pet 3:18–22); Peter draws a typological connection between Noah's deliverance through water and baptism.
speaker: Peter
summary: Baptism saves you — as an appeal to God for a good conscience, through Christ's resurrection.

gk_keywords: kosmos; emploke; peribolē; praus; hesychios pneuma
interpretive_notes: The inner/outer contrast is the emphasis; most interpreters read this as redirecting values rather than issuing an absolute prohibition on hairstyling or jewelry.
literary_context: 1 Pet 3:1-6 — advice to wives; part of the household code; the dress instruction follows the submission command.
q2_notes: Modesty principle applied without enforcing specific cultural prohibitions.
speaker: Peter (apostolic letter)
summary: True adornment is inward (gentle spirit) not outward (braided hair, gold, fine clothes).

interpretive_notes: The 'weaker vessel' phrase has been variously interpreted (physical, social, or situational); the co-heir clause asserts spiritual equality. The prayer-hindrance warning is unique.
literary_context: 1 Pet 3 household code; follows the extensive wife command (3:1-6); notable for the spiritual-equality clause.
q2_notes: "Weaker vessel" is interpreted variously — physical weakness, social vulnerability, or ontological distinction. The co-heir clause ("heirs together of the grace of life") is frequently underemphasized relative to the weakness clause.
speaker: Peter (apostolic letter)
summary: Husbands to live with wives with understanding, honoring them as co-heirs of grace, or prayers will be hindered.

interpretive_notes: Provides evangelistic motivation; the submission is instrumentalised toward the husband's conversion.
literary_context: 1 Peter 2:11-3:12 household code; immediately follows servant command (2:18); wives addressed before husbands (3:7).
q2_notes: Explicitly evangelistic purpose (C2=Y: purpose is winning the husband). The same Purpose–Form logic used here — that the form serves a purpose that could be achieved differently — is not applied to the submission command itself.
speaker: Peter (apostolic letter)
summary: Wives told to submit so that unbelieving husbands may be won to faith by behavior rather than words.

category: A8 — Welcome the stranger / show hospitality
interpretive_notes: Hospitality and service framed as stewardship of grace; love covers sin through practical mercy.
literary_context: Living as stewards of grace (1 Pet 4:7–11); closing community ethics; love, hospitality, and service are primary.
speaker: Peter
summary: Love earnestly, show hospitality without grumbling, serve one another — as stewards of God's grace.

interpretive_notes: Two linked imperatives: humble and cast. The anxiety-casting is an act of trust that flows from humility. Ps 55:22 provides the OT antecedent. Among the most-quoted pastoral encouragement texts.
literary_context: 1 Peter 5; closing exhortations to the community; follows instruction to young people (5:5) and precedes the devil warning (5:8-9).
speaker: Peter (apostolic letter)
summary: Humble yourselves under the mighty hand of God so that he may exalt you at the proper time — casting all your anxieties on him, because he cares for you.

category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
interpretive_notes: [RESEARCHER TO COMPLETE]
literary_context: Closing exhortations of 1 Peter; follows instruction to elders and the young (5:1-7).
speaker: Peter (apostolic letter)
summary: Be sober-minded and watchful — your adversary the devil prowls like a roaring lion. Resist him, firm in your faith.

interpretive_notes: The anti-domineering command (me katakyrieuvontes) directly limits the authority of the elder; servant leadership is the paradigm.
literary_context: 1 Pet 5:1-11 — closing instructions to elders and the whole congregation; Peter addresses both leaders and members.
q2_notes: The shepherding metaphor and humility ethic are universally affirmed. "Not lording it over" is widely preached.
speaker: Peter (apostolic letter)
summary: Elders to shepherd willingly, eagerly, not for money, not by dominating; young people to submit; all clothe in humility.

interpretive_notes: The remarkable word theodidaktos ('God-taught') grounds the love command in divine instruction rather than apostolic authority.
literary_context: 1 Thess 4:1-12 — ethical instructions; Paul affirms and reinforces the Thessalonians' practice of mutual love.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: You have been taught by God to love one another — continue to do so more and more.

interpretive_notes: Sanctification is explicitly named as God's will; holiness is not peripheral but God's stated purpose.
literary_context: Instructions in holiness (1 Thess 4:1–8); Paul names sanctification as God's explicit will.
speaker: Paul
summary: This is the will of God — your sanctification; abstain from sexual immorality.

interpretive_notes: Missionary testimony rationale: orderly, self-sufficient Christian life is a witness to outsiders.
literary_context: 1 Thess 4:9-12 — brotherly love and orderly conduct; part of ethical exhortation before the eschatological section.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Aspire to a quiet life, mind your own affairs, work with your hands, be dependent on no one.

category: A11 — Bear one another's burdens / help the weak
interpretive_notes: The weak are to be actively helped, not judged; patience is the posture toward all categories of vulnerability.
literary_context: Final exhortations (1 Thess 5:12–22); closing ethical commands; care for the vulnerable is listed alongside doctrinal faithfulness.
speaker: Paul
summary: Encourage the fainthearted, help the weak — be patient with them all.

interpretive_notes: The most compact prayer command in the NT; unceasing prayer as a life posture, not a single act.
literary_context: Final exhortations (1 Thess 5:16–22); one of the briefest commands in the NT.
speaker: Paul
summary: Pray without ceasing.

interpretive_notes: One of five NT occurrences.
literary_context: 1 Thess 5:12-28 — closing instructions; immediately follows commands on prophecy and discernment.
q2_notes: Third occurrence — in the same letter as 1 Thess 5:17 (pray without ceasing) which is universally applied.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Greet all the brothers with a holy kiss.

gk_keywords: sbennymi (quench); pneuma (Spirit); prophēteias (prophecies); exoutheneō (despise); dokimazō (test); kalos (good); ponēros (evil)
interpretive_notes: Four rapid commands governing charismatic worship. The tension between openness (do not quench, do not despise) and discernment (test, abstain) is deliberately maintained.
literary_context: 1 Thessalonians 5; rapid-fire closing exhortations; follows "rejoice, pray, give thanks" (5:16-18).
q2_notes: vv.21-22 ("test everything; hold fast what is good") are universally affirmed. But vv.19-20 ("do not quench the Spirit; do not despise prophecies") are effectively ignored or reinterpreted by cessationist traditions — creating a hermeneutical asymmetry within the same passage.
q2_typical_application: SELECTIVELY APPLIED (vv.21-22 universally affirmed; vv.19-20 vary by tradition)
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Do not quench the Spirit. Do not despise prophecies, but test everything; hold fast what is good, abstain from every form of evil.

gk_keywords: chairō; adialeiptōs; proseuchē; eucharistō; thelema Theou
interpretive_notes: The three commands are co-ordinated and declared to be 'the will of God in Christ Jesus' — a rare explicit statement that the will of God equals a specific ethical practice.
literary_context: 1 Thess 5:12-22 — rapid ethical commands; these three are the densest cluster of short commands in the epistles.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Always rejoice, pray without ceasing, give thanks in all circumstances — this is God's will.

interpretive_notes: The ground for respect is their work, not merely their office — a functionally based rather than purely hierarchical view of leadership honor.
literary_context: 1 Thess 5:12-22 — rapid-fire closing ethical exhortations.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Respect those who lead and admonish you; hold them in highest regard in love.

gk_keywords: deēsis (supplication); proseuchē (prayer); enteuxis (intercession); eucharistia (thanksgiving); hyper pantōn (for all)
interpretive_notes: The four-fold prayer vocabulary (supplication, prayer, intercession, thanksgiving) covers all modes; 'for all people' including rulers is the breadth the passage demands.
literary_context: 1 Tim 2:1-7 — prayer for all people and rulers; the first priority of the gathered church is comprehensive intercessory prayer.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Urge that prayers of all kinds be made for all people — kings and those in authority.

interpretive_notes: Authenteō (translated 'authority') occurs only here in NT; its meaning is disputed (domineering vs. normal authority). Contextual debates: whether this addresses a specific Ephesian problem or states universal norms.
literary_context: 1 Tim 2:8-15 — worship instructions; Paul addresses women specifically after addressing men.
q2_notes: CRITICAL CASE: C1=P: v.13 appeals to Adam being formed first (creation order — the strongest evangelical argument for universality). C3=P: some scholars argue Paul is addressing a specific Ephesian problem (women promoting false teaching). C4=N: v.15 ("she will be saved through childbearing") is in the same pericope — if vv.11-12 are binding, v.15 demands equal weight. C5=N: systematic exclusion of women from teaching creates structural tension with mercy-inclusion commands (A10, A12).
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Women to learn in quietness; Paul does not permit women to teach or exercise authority over men.

gk_keywords: proseuchomai; epairō; hosias cheiras; orge; dialogismos
interpretive_notes: The physical posture (raised hands) was common in ancient prayer; the moral qualifiers ('holy hands, without anger') may be the primary emphasis rather than the posture itself.
literary_context: 1 Tim 2:1-15 — instructions on prayer and conduct in worship; Paul gives directions to both men (v.8) and women (vv.9-15).
q2_notes: CRITICAL CASE: This verse immediately precedes 1 Tim 2:11-12 (women not to teach). It has the same passage type, the same author, the same contextual unit. The commanded posture for male prayer (hands raised) is almost universally ignored. If 2:11-12 is binding, 2:8 is equally so. The selective application of one verse and not the other in the same pericope is the clearest example of hermeneutical inconsistency in the NT.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Men instructed to pray with lifted holy hands, free from anger and dispute.

gk_keywords: kosmios; aidōs; sophrosynē; ploke; chrysos; margarites
interpretive_notes: The contrast is between costly, status-displaying ornamentation and modest/respectable dress. Whether specific items (braids, gold) are prohibited absolutely or only as status symbols is debated.
literary_context: 1 Tim 2:8-15 — worship conduct instructions; follows the command to men (v.8).
q2_notes: The modesty principle is affirmed but the specific prohibitions (braided hair, gold, pearls, costly attire) are universally ignored. Adjacent verse 1 Tim 2:11-12 is treated as binding — a hermeneutical asymmetry within the same passage.
q2_typical_application: SELECTIVE (modesty principle retained; specific prohibitions ignored)
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Women to dress modestly with self-control, not with elaborate braided hair, gold, pearls, or expensive clothing.

interpretive_notes: The emphasis throughout is character and household management; the only competency mentioned is teaching ability. The 'husband of one wife' phrase is debated (polygamy prohibition? remarriage? female overseers?).
literary_context: 1 Tim 3:1-13 — qualifications for overseers and deacons; part of the pastoral letter's church-order instructions.
q2_notes: The passage lists 15+ qualifications but institutional practice focuses narrowly on 2-3 (especially "husband of one wife"). Qualifications like hospitality and gentleness receive far less scrutiny.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Overseers must meet a comprehensive character profile: blameless, one wife, temperate, hospitable, able to teach, financially unattached.

interpretive_notes: The mention of 'their wives' (or 'women deacons' — gynaikas is ambiguous) in v.11 is a key text in debates about female deacons.
literary_context: 1 Tim 3 — deacon qualifications immediately follow overseer qualifications; the two lists are parallel in structure.
q2_notes: Deacon qualifications are cited but applied with even more variation than elder qualifications. The role of women deacons (v.11) is the primary point of contention.
q2_typical_application: SELECTIVE (deacon qualifications cited but applied with wide variation)
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Deacons must be dignified, honest, temperate, hold the faith with a clear conscience, tested, one spouse, managing household well.

gk_keywords: kataphroneō (despise); neotēs (youth); typos (example/pattern); logos (speech); anastrophē (conduct); agapē (love); pistis (faith); hagneia (purity)
interpretive_notes: Age is overcome by exemplary character. Five domains of example-setting cover the whole of public and private life.
literary_context: 1 Timothy 4; Paul's charge to Timothy regarding his conduct and ministry; follows warning about false teachers.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Let no one despise you for your youth — set the believers an example in speech, in conduct, in love, in faith, in purity.

category: A7 — Care for widows and orphans
interpretive_notes: This passage shows that early church care for widows was institutionally organized; Paul's primary principle is that the church is a backstop, not a replacement for family care.
literary_context: 1 Tim 5:3-16 — detailed instructions on widows in the church; qualifications for the widow-list, warnings about younger widows, and obligations of believing families.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Enroll genuine widows aged 60+ on the support list; families must care for their own widows.

category: A7 — Care for widows and orphans
interpretive_notes: Church and family both bear responsibility for widows; failure to provide is equated with apostasy — the strongest possible language.
literary_context: Instructions about widows (1 Tim 5:1–16); Timothy is given detailed instructions for the church's care of widows.
speaker: Paul
summary: Honor widows who are truly widows — failing to provide for family is to deny the faith.

interpretive_notes: The 'double honor' is widely interpreted as both respect and financial remuneration (supported by the agricultural and wage citations).
literary_context: 1 Tim 5:17-25 — instructions on treatment of elders; honor, pay, and protection from false accusation.
q2_notes: Both the honor principle and the protection of elders from unsubstantiated accusation are widely affirmed and consistently practiced.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Elders who lead well deserve double honor; don't accept accusations against an elder without two or three witnesses.

audience: Families of widows / heads of households
interpretive_notes: Context is specifically care for widowed relatives so the church's resources can focus on those with no family. The strong language ('denied the faith, worse than an unbeliever') indicates high gravity.
literary_context: 1 Tim 5:3-16 — instructions on care for widows; Paul establishes that families must care for their own widows first.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Provide for your own family; failing to do so is worse than being an unbeliever.

gk_keywords: eumetadotos (generous/ready to share); koinōnikous (ready to share)
interpretive_notes: Direct pastoral instruction to wealthy believers; generosity is the antidote to wealth's spiritual dangers.
literary_context: Final instructions (1 Tim 6:17–19); Paul gives Timothy a direct command to pass on to wealthy church members.
speaker: Paul
summary: Charge the rich to be generous and ready to share — storing treasure in heaven.

category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
gk_keywords: diōke (pursue); dikaiosynē (righteousness); agōnizou (fight); kalē agōna (good fight); epilabou (take hold); zōē aiōnios (eternal life)
interpretive_notes: Triple command structure: pursue, fight, take hold. The athletic/military metaphor combines with virtues. "Good fight" (kalos agōn) suggests nobility, not mere struggle.
literary_context: 1 Timothy 6; Paul's final charge to Timothy; follows the love-of-money warning (6:9-10) and precedes the doxology.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Pursue righteousness, godliness, faith, love, steadfastness, gentleness. Fight the good fight of the faith; take hold of the eternal life to which you were called.

category: B8 — Guard sound doctrine / contend for the faith
gk_keywords: phylassō (guard); parathēkē (deposit/entrusted thing); kenophōnia (empty babble); pseudōnymos gnōsis (falsely called knowledge)
interpretive_notes: parathēkē ('the deposit') is the technical term for something left in trust; Timothy is both guardian and steward of the apostolic teaching.
literary_context: 1 Tim 6:20-21 — the closing personal charge to Timothy; 'guard the deposit' is the final summation of the letter's doctrinal concerns.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Guard the deposit entrusted to you; avoid godless chatter and false knowledge.

gk_keywords: philargyria (love of money); rhiza (root); kakōn (evils); oregō (crave/desire); apoplanaō (wander away)
interpretive_notes: Often misquoted as 'money is the root of all evil'; Paul's actual statement is 'the love of money' (philargyria) is a root of 'all kinds of evils' (pantōn tōn kakōn). The love, not possession, is the danger.
literary_context: 1 Tim 6:6-19 — contentment, wealth warning, and instruction to the rich; the love-of-money warning is the negative counterpart to 6:6-8's contentment command.
q2_notes: Widely quoted but rarely applied with the institutional weight given to sexual ethics commands of comparable directness.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: The desire to be rich leads to destruction; the love of money is a root of all kinds of evil.

interpretive_notes: Adds a missiological/reputational rationale: poor conduct by Christian slaves would discredit the gospel.
literary_context: Pastoral letter instruction; part of Paul's social-order teaching in 1 Tim 5-6.
q2_notes: Paul grounds this in witness and the honor of God's name — not in social order. The command is structurally identical to 1 Tim 2:11-12 but is treated oppositely.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Enslaved persons told to honor masters so that God's name is not dishonored; especially to serve believing masters well.

interpretive_notes: This passage is often underused in evangelical financial teaching; it directly commands the wealthy to redistribute through generosity rather than merely giving a tithe.
literary_context: 1 Tim 6 — the second passage addressed to the wealthy; the first (vv.6-10) warns against love of money; this passage directs the already-wealthy.
q2_notes: This is a direct command to the rich — a demographic well-represented in evangelical churches. It is rarely preached as a binding command equivalent to the sexual ethics commands. The command's relative neglect while less wealthy-targeted commands are enforced suggests social-interest selection rather than hermeneutical consistency.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: The rich are charged: not proud, not trust in wealth, but be generous and ready to share.

interpretive_notes: Paul does not command poverty; he commands contentment regardless of circumstance (cf. Phil 4:11 — learned contentment).
literary_context: 1 Tim 6:3-10 — Paul's treatment of false teachers motivated by financial gain; the contentment command is the positive counterpart to the love-of-money warning.
q2_notes: The contentment principle is universally affirmed, but the specific standard ("food and clothing" as sufficient) is effectively ignored in prosperity-oriented church culture. The passage functions as a corrective to greed but its plain-sense material threshold is not applied.
q2_typical_application: SELECTIVELY APPLIED (contentment affirmed; material sufficiency standard ignored)
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Be content with food and clothing; godliness with contentment is great gain.

gk_keywords: aichmalōtizontes (taking captive); logismos (thought/argument); hypakoē (obedience); hypsoumenon (exalting itself)
interpretive_notes: [RESEARCHER TO COMPLETE]
literary_context: Paul's defense of his apostolic authority (chapters 10–13); responds to charges of weakness and inconsistency from opponents in Corinth.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: We demolish arguments and every lofty opinion raised against the knowledge of God, and take every thought captive to obey Christ.

gk_keywords: chairete (rejoice); katartizō (be restored); parakaleō (be encouraged); auto phroneō (agree); eirēneuō (live in peace)
interpretive_notes: Five rapid imperatives summarizing the community life Paul has been urging throughout 2 Corinthians. Reconciliation is the overarching theme.
literary_context: 2 Corinthians 13; closing valediction; after Paul's defense of apostolic authority.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Rejoice, aim for restoration, comfort one another, agree with one another, live in peace — and the God of love and peace will be with you.

interpretive_notes: Widely applied to marriage with non-Christians (cf. 1 Cor 7:39: 'marry only in the Lord'), but the original context may be broader — pagan cultic meals or business partnerships. Application to marriage is inferential.
literary_context: 2 Cor 6:14-7:1 — a sharp exhortation to separation; some scholars view this as a digression; others as the climax of Paul's appeal for reconciliation.
ot_antecedent: Deut 22:10 (do not yoke ox and donkey together); Lev 19:19
q2_notes: Commonly applied exclusively to marriage despite the passage's broader context (the temple of God, 6:16). The narrowing of application to marriage while ignoring business partnerships is selective.
q2_typical_application: SELECTIVE (applied to marriage; ignored in business)
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers.

interpretive_notes: Qualitative distinction between genuine and superficial repentance; godly sorrow is the path to life.
literary_context: Paul's relief at the Corinthians' response (2 Cor 7:5–13); Paul distinguishes genuine repentance from mere sorrow.
speaker: Paul
summary: Godly grief produces repentance leading to salvation — worldly grief produces death.

gk_keywords: katharizō (cleanse); molysmē (defilement); hagiōsynē (holiness); epitelesō (bring to completion)
interpretive_notes: The command is self-directed (katharisōmen heautous — let us cleanse ourselves), not passive; holiness is both imputed (through Christ) and pursued (through self-cleansing).
literary_context: 2 Cor 6:14–7:1 — the call to separation from unbelief; a concluding exhortation grounded in the temple-indwelling promises of 6:16-18.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Cleanse yourselves from every defilement of body and spirit, completing holiness in the fear of God.

interpretive_notes: The incarnation is the theological foundation of economic generosity; Christological grounding of wealth-sharing.
literary_context: Appeal for the Jerusalem collection (2 Cor 8); Paul grounds generosity in the incarnation of Christ.
speaker: Paul
summary: Though rich, Christ became poor for our sake — his poverty is the model for our generosity.

interpretive_notes: Poverty + joy + generosity as the model; they gave themselves first (v.5); grace (charis) is the word used for their giving.
literary_context: Appeal for the Jerusalem collection (2 Cor 8–9); Paul holds up the Macedonian churches as models of sacrificial giving.
speaker: Paul
summary: In severe poverty and great joy the Macedonians gave beyond their means — out of their own accord.

category: A6 — Pursue economic equality / oppose exploitation
interpretive_notes: Quotes Exod 16:18 (manna); abundance and lack should balance out; equality is the stated NT economic goal.
literary_context: Appeal for the Jerusalem collection (2 Cor 8); Paul frames the collection as seeking economic equality between churches.
speaker: Paul
summary: Your abundance should supply their need — economic equality as the explicit goal.

category: A3 — Give to the needy / almsgiving
interpretive_notes: Giving from free decision, not compulsion; joyfulness is the posture; quotes Prov 22:8 LXX.
literary_context: Second appeal for the collection (2 Cor 9); Paul encourages voluntary, joyful giving.
speaker: Paul
summary: Each one must give as decided in his heart — not reluctantly, for God loves a cheerful giver.

category: A3 — Give to the needy / almsgiving
interpretive_notes: Generosity as doxology; the goal of giving is not the gift but the glory of God through it.
literary_context: Second appeal for the collection (2 Cor 9); Paul describes how generosity glorifies God and produces thanksgiving.
speaker: Paul
summary: You are enriched in every way to be generous — generosity produces thanksgiving and glorifies God.

gk_keywords: agapaō; entolē; ap' archēs (from the beginning)
interpretive_notes: The insistence on 'not a new commandment' answers those who might regard the love command as a Johannine innovation.
literary_context: 2 John 1:4-6 — John emphasizes that the love command is ancient, not novel; contextualised against false teachers in the community.
speaker: John (Elder)
summary: Love one another — not a new commandment but the one from the beginning.

category: B8 — Guard sound doctrine / contend for the faith
interpretive_notes: The most exclusionary doctrinal command in John's letters; hospitality is restricted based on doctrinal faithfulness.
literary_context: Warning about false teachers (2 John 7–11); itinerant teachers who deny the incarnation are not to be welcomed.
speaker: John
subtheme: Abide in the Teaching of Christ
summary: Whoever does not abide in Christ's teaching does not have God — do not receive false teachers.

interpretive_notes: In the house-church context, receiving a teacher was to endorse and fund their ministry. Not extending hospitality was a formal withdrawal of support, not personal rudeness.
literary_context: 2 John 7-11 — the elder warns against receiving travelling false teachers (those who deny the incarnation) into home-based churches.
q2_notes: Context is itinerant false teachers, not local members with different views.
speaker: The Elder (John)
summary: Do not welcome into your home or greet anyone who comes with a teaching other than Christ's.

gk_keywords: spoudē (diligence); aretē (virtue); gnōsis (knowledge); enkrateia (self-control); eusebeia (godliness); philadelphia (brotherly affection); agapē (love)
interpretive_notes: The "virtue ladder" — seven qualities building on faith and climaxing in love. The structure suggests progressive Christian maturity, not sequential stages.
literary_context: 2 Peter 1; follows the divine-nature promises (1:3-4); the virtue ladder leads to the "make your calling sure" exhortation (1:10).
speaker: Peter (apostolic letter)
summary: Make every effort to supplement your faith with virtue, virtue with knowledge, knowledge with self-control, self-control with steadfastness, steadfastness with godliness, godliness with brotherly affection, and brotherly affection with love.

category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
gk_keywords: spoudazō (be diligent); bebaian (sure/firm); klēsis (calling); eklogē (election); ptaiō (stumble)
interpretive_notes: Not about earning election but about confirming it through visible growth. "Never stumble" is the promised result of diligent virtue development.
literary_context: 2 Peter 1; follows the virtue ladder (1:5-7); the practical result of growing in godliness.
speaker: Peter (apostolic letter)
summary: Be all the more diligent to confirm your calling and election — for if you practice these qualities you will never stumble.

interpretive_notes: Divine patience is oriented toward universal repentance; the delay of judgment is an act of mercy toward the unrepentant.
literary_context: The day of the Lord (2 Pet 3:1–13); Peter addresses mockers who question the delay of Christ's return.
speaker: Peter
summary: God is patient — not wanting any to perish but all to come to repentance.

gk_keywords: phylassō (be on guard); phero (carried away); athesmōn (lawless); planē (error); auxanō (grow); charis (grace); gnōsis (knowledge)
interpretive_notes: Dual closing imperatives: guard (defensive) and grow (positive). Knowledge of Christ is the antidote to doctrinal instability.
literary_context: 2 Peter 3; closing exhortation; follows the day-of-the-Lord passage and Paul reference (3:14-16).
speaker: Peter (apostolic letter)
summary: Be on your guard lest you be carried away with the error of lawless people and lose your own stability. But grow in the grace and knowledge of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ.

category: B8 — Guard sound doctrine / contend for the faith
gk_keywords: stēkete (stand firm); krateite (hold to); paradoseis (traditions); logos (word); epistolē (letter)
interpretive_notes: Key text for apostolic tradition — both oral and written. "Traditions" (paradoseis) refers to authoritative apostolic teaching, not human customs.
literary_context: 2 Thessalonians 2; after the man of lawlessness discourse; Paul urges the church to stand firm amid eschatological confusion.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Stand firm and hold to the traditions that you were taught by us, whether by our spoken word or by our letter.

audience: Idle members of the Thessalonian church
interpretive_notes: The eschatological context: some had stopped working expecting the imminent parousia. Paul's instruction grounds physical provision in personal responsibility.
literary_context: 2 Thess 3:6-15 — the idle-members passage; Paul appeals to his own example of manual labor.
q2_notes: C4=N: The passage addresses a specific eschatological idleness (people quitting work because Christ was returning imminently) — not general poverty. Consistently applied to those receiving public assistance while rarely applied to the wealthy who live off inherited wealth or passive income.
q2_typical_application: SELECTIVE (applied to recipients of welfare; rarely to idle wealthy)
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: If unwilling to work, do not eat; idle members commanded to work quietly and earn their own bread.

interpretive_notes: Context is likely eschatological idleness: some stopped working because they believed the Lord's return was imminent.
literary_context: 2 Thess 3:6-15 — Paul's command concerning idle members who were not working; part of his closing instructions.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Keep away from any believer living in idleness contrary to apostolic teaching.

interpretive_notes: The restorative framing ('warn as a brother') qualifies the withdrawal; this is discipline for correction, not rejection.
literary_context: 2 Thess 3 — conclusion of the discipline passage on idleness.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Mark and avoid the disobedient person so they are ashamed, but treat them as a brother not an enemy.

category: B8 — Guard sound doctrine / contend for the faith
interpretive_notes: Sound doctrine is a deposit to be guarded; the Holy Spirit is the guardian's resource.
literary_context: Paul's charge to Timothy (2 Tim 1:8–14); Paul urges Timothy to hold to the apostolic deposit.
speaker: Paul
summary: Follow the pattern of sound words and guard the good deposit entrusted to you.

category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
gk_keywords: synkakopathō (share in suffering); stratiōtēs (soldier); Christos Iēsous
interpretive_notes: syn- prefix ('together') is significant — Paul calls Timothy to shared suffering, not isolated stoicism; the soldier image emphasizes disciplined focus.
literary_context: 2 Tim 2:1-7 — three images of faithful endurance: soldier, athlete, farmer; Paul urges Timothy toward endurance through difficulty.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Endure hardship together as a good soldier of Christ Jesus.

category: B8 — Guard sound doctrine / contend for the faith
gk_keywords: spoudazō (be diligent/do your best); dokimos (approved); orthotomounta (rightly dividing/handling); alētheia (truth)
interpretive_notes: The classic pastoral charge for faithful Bible teaching. "Rightly handling" (orthotomounta) literally means "cutting straight" — accuracy in interpretation.
literary_context: 2 Timothy 2; Paul's charge to Timothy amid suffering; between the faithful-sayings and the false-teaching warnings.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Do your best to present yourself to God as one approved, a worker who has no need to be ashamed, rightly handling the word of truth.

gk_keywords: pheuge (flee); neōterikas epithymias (youthful passions); diōke (pursue); dikaiosynē (righteousness); pistis (faith); agapē (love); eirēnē (peace)
interpretive_notes: Dual imperative: flee and pursue. The Christian life is both escape from and movement toward. The communal element ("along with those who call on the Lord") is striking.
literary_context: 2 Timothy 2; Paul's instructions to Timothy; follows the vessels analogy (2:20-21).
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Flee youthful passions and pursue righteousness, faith, love, and peace, along with those who call on the Lord from a pure heart.

category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
gk_keywords: nēphō (be sober); kakopathēson (endure suffering); euangelistēs (evangelist); plērophorēson (fulfill)
interpretive_notes: Four imperatives defining Timothy's calling as minister; 'do the work of an evangelist' also belongs to B9 (this verse cross-applies to multiple categories).
literary_context: 2 Tim 4:1-5 — Paul's final charge to Timothy; the coming apostasy is described, and Timothy is called to faithfulness regardless.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Be sober, endure suffering, do the work of an evangelist, fulfill your ministry.

gk_keywords: kēryxon (preach); logos (word); eukairos / akairos (in/out of season)
interpretive_notes: Pastoral urgency; preaching is non-negotiable regardless of circumstances; four specific acts: preach, reprove, rebuke, exhort.
literary_context: Paul's final charge to Timothy (2 Tim 4:1–8); Paul's last instructions before his death.
speaker: Paul
subtheme: Preach the Word in Season and Out
summary: Preach the word — in season and out of season; reprove, rebuke, and exhort.

category: A8 — Welcome the stranger / show hospitality
interpretive_notes: Hospitality to strangers and travelling gospel workers is active support, not merely shelter; 'worthy of God' sets the standard.
literary_context: Commendation of Gaius (3 John 1–8); John commends a believer for supporting travelling missionaries and strangers.
speaker: John
summary: You are faithful in what you do for brothers and strangers — send them on in a manner worthy of God.

interpretive_notes: Concentric geographic mission; Spirit-empowered witness; provides the structural outline for the book of Acts.
literary_context: Ascension (Acts 1:1–11); final words of Jesus before ascending; the Spirit empowers witness.
speaker: Jesus
subtheme: Witness to the End of the Earth
summary: You will be my witnesses — in Jerusalem, Judea, Samaria, and to the end of the earth.

category: A3 — Give to the needy / almsgiving
interpretive_notes: Almsgiving treated as prayer that rises to God; a Gentile's mercy shapes the course of redemptive history.
literary_context: Cornelius vision (Acts 10:1–8); Roman centurion receives angelic confirmation; his prayers and alms are described as reaching God.
speaker: Narrator / Angel
summary: Cornelius gave alms generously — his alms and prayers ascended as a memorial before God.

interpretive_notes: The passive reception of the Spirit before baptism (reversing the usual order) establishes that baptism follows faith, not produces it; yet it remains commanded.
literary_context: Acts 10:44-48 — the conversion of Cornelius's household; the Spirit falls before baptism, and Peter commands baptism as the proper response.
speaker: Peter (apostolic command)
summary: Peter commands the Gentile believers to be baptized in the name of Jesus Christ.

category: A2 — Feed the hungry / provide for bodily needs
interpretive_notes: First recorded inter-church financial mercy collection; 'according to ability' is proportional, not uniform.
literary_context: Antioch church response to famine prophecy (Acts 11:27–30); first inter-church mercy collection; Barnabas and Saul carry it.
speaker: Narrator
summary: The Antioch church sends relief to Judean brothers during famine — each giving as able.

interpretive_notes: [RESEARCHER TO COMPLETE]
literary_context: Paul and Barnabas's first missionary journey; returning through previously visited cities to strengthen churches.
q2_notes: Church polity is not creation-grounded. The purpose (qualified leadership) is universal, but the specific elder structure is one organizational model among several. Many churches affirm elders in principle but actual structures vary widely.
q2_typical_application: SELECTIVELY APPLIED (cited by elder-led churches; ignored by others)
speaker: Luke (Acts narrator)
summary: Paul and Barnabas appointed elders in every church, committing them to the Lord with prayer and fasting.

audience: Gentile believers in Antioch, Syria, Cilicia
interpretive_notes: The decree is the earliest recorded formal decision of the church as a body; still binding? Most Protestant traditions treat only the sexual immorality clause as universally normative.
literary_context: Acts 15 — the formal letter from the Jerusalem Council to Gentile churches; repeats the decree of v.20.
q2_notes: See Acts 15:20.
speaker: Jerusalem Council (apostles and elders)
summary: Written decree: abstain from food sacrificed to idols, blood, strangled animals, and sexual immorality.

interpretive_notes: The Jerusalem Decree is unique in the NT as a formal conciliar decision. The four prohibitions likely track Levitical laws governing resident aliens (Lev 17-18), enabling Jewish-Gentile table fellowship.
literary_context: Acts 15:1-29 — Jerusalem Council; decision on Gentile conversion requirements; James proposes these four abstentions.
q2_notes: CRITICAL CASE: The Jerusalem Council decree — one of the most formal decisions of the apostolic church. The sexual immorality clause is universally applied; the food clauses (including abstaining from blood) are universally ignored. Application of this decree is entirely selective.
speaker: James / Jerusalem Council
summary: Gentile believers to abstain from food offered to idols, sexual immorality, strangled animals, and blood.

category: B2 — Believe / have faith in Christ
interpretive_notes: The simplest NT formulation of saving faith; household salvation included; response to the most urgent existential question.
literary_context: Philippian jailer (Acts 16:25–34); earthquake; jailer asks 'What must I do to be saved?'; Paul and Silas answer.
speaker: Paul / Silas
summary: Believe in the Lord Jesus and you will be saved.

interpretive_notes: Universal scope; not invitation but command; addressed to philosophers outside Jewish tradition.
literary_context: Areopagus sermon (Acts 17:22–31); Paul addresses Athenian philosophers; repentance is commanded universally to all people.
speaker: Paul
summary: God now commands all people everywhere to repent.

interpretive_notes: Voluntary redistribution of wealth as the economic expression of Pentecost; 'as any had need' is need-based, not equal-distribution.
literary_context: Pentecost community summary (Acts 2:42–47); describes the economic life of the Jerusalem church immediately after the Spirit's coming.
speaker: Narrator
summary: All who believed had all things in common — selling possessions to distribute to any who had need.

interpretive_notes: The three-part apostolic call: repentance, baptism, Spirit. First proclamation after Pentecost.
literary_context: Pentecost sermon (Acts 2:14–41); crowd asks 'What shall we do?'; Peter gives the definitive apostolic answer.
speaker: Peter
summary: Repent and be baptized — every one of you — for forgiveness and the gift of the Spirit.

interpretive_notes: Baptism is the expected next step after repentance; linked to forgiveness and gift of Spirit.
literary_context: Pentecost sermon (Acts 2:14–41); also appears under B1 — here the focus is on baptism as commanded act.
speaker: Peter
summary: Be baptized in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of sins.

category: A3 — Give to the needy / almsgiving
interpretive_notes: Only Pauline citation of a Jesus saying not in the Gospels; giving and helping the weak are grounded in Jesus' own words.
literary_context: Paul's farewell to the Ephesian elders (Acts 20:17–38); Paul cites Jesus' words as the basis for his practice of giving.
speaker: Paul
summary: Help the weak — it is more blessed to give than to receive, as Jesus said.

gk_keywords: prosechō; poimne; episkopos; poimaino; ekklesia tou Theou
interpretive_notes: Paul attributes oversight appointments to the Holy Spirit, not merely congregational vote or apostolic appointment.
literary_context: Acts 20:17-38 — Paul's farewell address to the Ephesian elders at Miletus; the most detailed pastoral charge in Acts.
speaker: Paul (in reported speech)
summary: Elders charged to watch over themselves and the flock; the Holy Spirit appointed them as overseers.

interpretive_notes: Urgency of baptism ('why do you wait?'); washing metaphor; personal address to Paul himself.
literary_context: Paul's Damascus road testimony (Acts 22:1–21); Ananias's words to Paul after his encounter with Christ.
speaker: Ananias
summary: Rise and be baptized — wash away your sins, calling on his name.

interpretive_notes: Two verbs: repent (internal change) and turn back (directional change); together they describe full conversion.
literary_context: Solomon's portico sermon (Acts 3:11–26); Peter preaches after healing the lame man.
speaker: Peter
summary: Repent and turn back — that your sins may be blotted out.

interpretive_notes: Echoes Deut 15:4 ('there will be no poor among you'); early church as Jubilee embodied. Need-elimination is the stated goal.
literary_context: Second Jerusalem community summary (Acts 4:32–37); follows the apostles' prayer and Spirit-filling; expands on the community economic life.
speaker: Narrator
summary: No one claimed private ownership — great grace was upon them; no one was needy.

category: A7 — Care for widows and orphans
interpretive_notes: The church organizes structurally around the mercy need; the diaconate office is born from widow-care failure.
literary_context: Appointment of the Seven (Acts 6:1–7); growth of the church creates an administrative mercy failure; the apostles create the diaconate to solve it.
speaker: Narrator
summary: Hellenist widows were being neglected — deacons appointed to ensure equitable distribution.

category: A7 — Care for widows and orphans
interpretive_notes: Mercy to widows is so central to her identity that her death triggers communal mourning; her works are her legacy.
literary_context: Raising of Tabitha (Acts 9:36–43); Tabitha dies; Peter is summoned; widows mourn showing her clothing gifts.
speaker: Narrator
summary: Tabitha was full of good works and acts of charity — she was mourned by the widows she had clothed.

category: B8 — Guard sound doctrine / contend for the faith
gk_keywords: peripateite (walk); blepete (see to it/beware); sylagōgeō (take captive); philosophia (philosophy); paradosis (tradition); stoicheia (elemental spirits)
interpretive_notes: Two commands: walk in Christ and beware of false philosophy. The Colossian "heresy" combined Jewish ritual with Hellenistic speculation.
literary_context: Colossians 2; bridge between the theological exposition and the ethical section; warns against the "Colossian heresy."
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: As you received Christ Jesus the Lord, walk in him, rooted and built up. See to it that no one takes you captive by philosophy and empty deceit according to human tradition.

interpretive_notes: charizomai ('grace to each other') is the forgiveness word here — rooted in undeserved grace; the Lord's forgiveness is the explicit model and ground.
literary_context: Col 3:12-17 — the 'put on' passage; a list of relational virtues climaxing in love; forgiveness is grounded in the Lord's prior forgiveness.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Bear with one another and forgive each other as the Lord has forgiven you.

interpretive_notes: Mercy virtues described as clothing to be deliberately put on; identity of the new person is fundamentally merciful.
literary_context: New life in Christ (Col 3:1–17); Paul describes the character of the renewed person; mercy virtues are listed as identity clothing.
speaker: Paul
summary: Put on compassion, kindness, humility, meekness, and patience — as God's chosen people.

gk_keywords: apotithēmi (put off); orgē (anger); blasphēmia (slander); aischros logos (filthy speech); endyō (put on); neos anthrōpos (new self)
interpretive_notes: The put-off/put-on metaphor (changing garments) structures Pauline ethics. The renewal is progressive and christological — toward the Creator's image.
literary_context: Colossians 3; the put-off/put-on section; between "put to death" (3:5) and "put on compassion" (3:12).
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Put off anger, wrath, malice, slander, and obscene talk. Put on the new self, which is being renewed in knowledge after the image of its creator.

category: B6 — Keep commandments / obey God's moral law
gk_keywords: pan ho ti (whatever); logos (word); ergon (deed); onoma kyriou Iēsou (name of the Lord Jesus); eucharistia (thanksgiving)
interpretive_notes: The Colossians parallel to 1 Cor 10:31. Covers the totality of life — speech and action — under Christ's lordship.
literary_context: Colossians 3:12-17; the put-on section; summary statement before the household codes (3:18ff).
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Whatever you do, in word or deed, do everything in the name of the Lord Jesus, giving thanks to God the Father through him.

gk_keywords: zēteite ta anō (seek the things above); phroneite (set your minds); dexia tou theou (right hand of God)
interpretive_notes: Two imperatives — seek and set your minds — grounded in the indicative of resurrection union with Christ. The ethical life flows from a reoriented worldview.
literary_context: Colossians 3 opens the ethical section; builds on the "raised with Christ" motif from 2:12-13.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: If you have been raised with Christ, seek the things that are above. Set your minds on things above, not on things on earth.

interpretive_notes: The prohibition on harshness (pikrainō) is practically significant; love is not merely emotional but behavioral.
literary_context: Household code in Col 3:18-4:1; brief parallel to Eph 5:25.
q2_notes: Broadly applied. The prohibition of harshness is underemphasized relative to the love command.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Husbands told to love wives and not be bitter/harsh toward them.

interpretive_notes: Parallel to Ephesians passage; adds eschatological sanction (inheritance/judgment).
literary_context: Household code section in Colossians (3:18-4:1); parallel to Eph 6:5-9.
q2_notes: See Eph 6:5-8. The same Purpose–Form logic (faithful work as service to Christ) that is applied here to justify recontextualisation is denied for other social-code commands.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Enslaved persons told to obey masters wholeheartedly, working as for Christ.

interpretive_notes: Simpler than Ephesians parallel; 'in everything' is broader but qualified by the 'in the Lord' framing of Eph 6:1.
literary_context: Household code in Col 3:18-4:1; parallel to Eph 6:1-3.
q2_notes: "In everything" is qualified implicitly by Christian obedience (cf. Acts 5:29).
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Children commanded to obey parents in everything as pleasing to the Lord.

interpretive_notes: The qualifier 'as is fitting in the Lord' suggests limits on the submission.
literary_context: Shorter parallel to Ephesians household code; part of Col 3:18-4:1.
q2_notes: Notably does NOT appeal to creation order — only "as is fitting in the Lord." The weaker grounding of this parallel suggests the submission command is not uniformly grounded across the NT.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Brief command for wives to submit to husbands 'as is fitting in the Lord'.

interpretive_notes: Uses erethizō (irritate/stir up) vs. parorgizō in Ephesians; the consequence here is discouragement rather than anger.
literary_context: Col 3:18-4:1 household code; parallel to Eph 6:4.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Fathers told not to provoke/irritate children lest they become discouraged.

gk_keywords: nekrōsate (put to death); porneia (sexual immorality); eidōlolatria (idolatry)
interpretive_notes: [RESEARCHER TO COMPLETE]
literary_context: Colossians 3:1-17 on putting off the old self and putting on the new; precedes the household codes (3:18ff).
q2_notes: Grounded explicitly in new life in Christ ("you have died, and your life is hidden with Christ in God"). Universal moral categories. Standard application is consistent.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Put to death what is earthly in you: sexual immorality, impurity, passion, evil desire, and covetousness — which is idolatry.

gk_keywords: logos tou Christou (word of Christ); enoikeō (dwell); didaskontes (teaching); nouthetountes (admonishing); psalmois, hymnois, ōdais (psalms, hymns, songs)
interpretive_notes: The word dwelling 'richly' (plousiōs) implies abundance, not minimal engagement; singing is a mode of mutual teaching, not merely personal expression.
literary_context: Col 3:15-17 — three worship commands: let peace rule, let the word dwell, do all in Jesus' name; 3:16 is the central liturgical instruction.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Let the word of Christ dwell richly among you — teach, admonish, and sing together with thankfulness.

interpretive_notes: proskartereō ('devote/persist') is strong — the same word used for the early church's prayer in Acts 2:42; watchfulness in prayer guards against distraction and formalism.
literary_context: Col 4:2-6 — closing exhortations; prayer for the mission and for open doors for the gospel.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Devote yourselves to prayer — keeping alert in it with thanksgiving.

interpretive_notes: Parallel to Eph 6:9; uses stronger justice language (to dikaion, isotēs — justice, equality).
