interpretive_notes: Identity statements with imperative force — "let your light shine" is the explicit command. Salt that loses saltiness is useless; a city on a hill cannot be hidden.
literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5-7); immediately after the Beatitudes; Jesus defines the mission-identity of his followers.
speaker: Jesus
summary: You are the salt of the earth and the light of the world — let your light shine before others, so they may see your good works and glorify your Father.

interpretive_notes: Holiness grounded in divine character; 'perfect' (teleios) means complete, mature, whole — especially in love.
literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5:43–48); closing statement of the antitheses; love of enemy is grounded in divine perfection.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Be perfect as your heavenly Father is perfect.

interpretive_notes: The internalization of the commandment is the key move: Jesus extends the prohibition from act to intent. The radical demand is meant to expose the inadequacy of external compliance.
literary_context: Sermon on the Mount antitheses (Matt 5:17-48); the lust command follows the anger/murder antithesis.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Looking at a person with lust constitutes adultery of the heart.

interpretive_notes: Exception clause (porneia) unique to Matthew; debated whether it refers to pre-marital unchastity or ongoing adultery.
literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5:17-48); one of the six 'You have heard... but I say' antitheses; follows prohibition on lust.
q2_notes: Grounded in creation (Matt 19:8 appeals to "from the beginning"). IC=P: consistently applied, but the exception clause is interpreted variously; some traditions add additional exceptions beyond porneia.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Divorcing a wife (except for sexual immorality) causes adultery; marrying a divorced woman is adultery.

interpretive_notes: Jesus radicalises the oath prohibition: the problem is not false swearing but swearing itself, because it implies a normal speech mode where one may be less than truthful. Plain speech is commanded.
literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5:33-37); the fourth antithesis; Jesus intensifies the prohibition against false oaths to a prohibition against oath-taking altogether.
q2_notes: CRITICAL CASE: In the Sermon on the Mount; same literary context as the Beatitudes and the love-of-enemy command (both widely applied). Explicitly dominical. Addresses a universal situation. C4=N: the prohibition of oath-taking is treated as cultural, but the surrounding antitheses are not — selective application within a single literary unit. Court oaths, pledge of allegiance, and membership oaths are universal in evangelical practice.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Do not swear oaths at all — let your yes be yes and your no be no.

interpretive_notes: Absolute statement with no qualifications; wealth-service is incompatible with God-service.
literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 6); concludes the section on treasure and anxiety; absolute statement on divided loyalty.
speaker: Jesus
summary: You cannot serve both God and money — loyalty is binary.

interpretive_notes: One of Jesus' sharpest statements on the mutuality of forgiveness; the condition is not merit but posture — the unforgiving heart is incompatible with receiving divine mercy.
literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 6); the immediate follow-on to the Lord's Prayer (6:9-13); Jesus unpacks the forgiveness petition.
speaker: Jesus
summary: God's forgiveness of you is contingent on your forgiveness of others.

category: A3 — Give to the needy / almsgiving
interpretive_notes: Jesus assumes almsgiving as standard disciple practice; the command is about motive and manner, not whether to give.
literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 6); triad of piety — almsgiving, prayer, fasting; Jesus assumes giving to the poor is a regular practice.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Give to the needy in secret, not for public praise — the Father who sees in secret will reward.

interpretive_notes: Debt language draws on Jubilee and Sabbath-year economic ethics; forgiveness of moral and economic debts are intertwined.
literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 6); Lord's Prayer; economic language used as moral metaphor for forgiveness.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Forgive our debts as we forgive our debtors — divine forgiveness conditioned on human mercy.

interpretive_notes: Kingdom inversion of economic priorities; hoarding wealth is directly counter-commanded.
literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 6); teaching on single-minded devotion; followed immediately by the lamp of the body and serving two masters.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Do not hoard earthly treasure — store treasure in heaven instead.

interpretive_notes: Private, unhypocritical prayer is commanded; the Father who sees in secret rewards.
literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 6:5–8); triad of piety — almsgiving, prayer, fasting; Jesus teaches about authentic prayer.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Pray in secret to your Father — not for public show.

interpretive_notes: The Lord's Prayer contains both doctrinal petition and mercy content (forgiveness of debts); a comprehensive model.
literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 6:9–13); the Lord's Prayer as a model for prayer.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Pray like this: Our Father... your kingdom come, your will be done. Daily bread. Forgive debts.

interpretive_notes: Jesus grounds the anti-anxiety command in creation theology and divine fatherhood; anxiety is the spiritual symptom of misaligned priorities ('seek first the kingdom', v.33). Moved here from D5 as a spiritual formation / holiness command rather than a worship-order instruction.
literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 6:19-34); follows the command on treasures and the lamp of the body; the anxiety prohibition is grounded in the Father's provision and kingdom priority.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Do not be anxious about food, drink, or clothing — life is more than these things.

interpretive_notes: One of the most frequently quoted NT commands. The prohibition is against hypocritical or self-righteous judgment, not moral discernment (cf. Matt 18:15-17). Luke's parallel adds "do not condemn."
literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5–7); following the Lord's Prayer and teaching on anxiety; transitions to the log-and-speck metaphor.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Do not judge, or you will be judged by the same measure — remove the log from your own eye before addressing the speck in your brother's.

interpretive_notes: The "Golden Rule." Jesus frames the positive formulation (do) rather than the negative (do not), elevating active love over passive avoidance. Hillel taught the negative form.
literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5–7); summarising statement before the narrow gate; Jesus says this sums up the Law and the Prophets.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Whatever you wish that others would do to you, do also to them — this is the Law and the Prophets.

category: B6 — Keep commandments / obey God's moral law
gk_keywords: akouō (hear); poieō (do); phronimos (wise); petra (rock); mōros (foolish); ammos (sand)
interpretive_notes: The parable functions as a summary command for the entire Sermon on the Mount: hearing without doing is catastrophic. The distinction is not knowledge but obedience.
literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5-7); the final parable; two builders as the climax and application of the entire sermon.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Everyone who hears these words and does them is like a wise man who built his house on the rock — the rain and floods came and it did not fall.

gk_keywords: eiserchomai (enter); stenē pylē (narrow gate); thlibō (narrow/difficult); hodos (way/road)
interpretive_notes: The two-ways motif has deep OT roots (Deut 30:15-20; Ps 1). Jesus makes the choice urgent and the stakes absolute.
literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5-7); conclusion section; the two ways — narrow gate vs. wide gate.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Enter through the narrow gate — for the gate is wide and the way is easy that leads to destruction, and many enter through it. The gate is narrow and the way is hard that leads to life.

category: B8 — Guard sound doctrine / contend for the faith
gk_keywords: prosechō (beware); pseudoprophētai (false prophets); endyma (clothing); probaton (sheep); lykos (wolf)
interpretive_notes: The test is by fruit, not appearance. Echoes Deut 13 and Ezek 22:27. Jesus warns about deception within the community, not external threats.
literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5-7); conclusion section; follows the narrow gate (7:13-14) and precedes "by their fruits" (7:16-20).
speaker: Jesus
summary: Beware of false prophets who come in sheep's clothing but inwardly are ravenous wolves.

interpretive_notes: Three present imperatives indicating continuous action. The promise is unconditional in form; context (7:9-11) grounds it in God's fatherly generosity.
literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5-7); between the judging prohibition (7:1-5) and the Golden Rule (7:12).
speaker: Jesus
summary: Ask, and it will be given to you; seek, and you will find; knock, and it will be opened — everyone who asks receives.

interpretive_notes: Hosea 6:6 quoted twice in Matthew; mercy-practice ranks above cultic obedience.
literary_context: Jesus eating with tax collectors at Matthew's house (Matt 9:9–13); Pharisees question his table fellowship; Jesus quotes Hosea 6:6.
speaker: Jesus
summary: God desires mercy, not sacrifice — Jesus defines his mission through prophetic mercy-priority.

gk_keywords: deesthē (pray/beseech); kyrios tou therismou (Lord of the harvest); ekballō (send out)
interpretive_notes: Notably the response to the needy crowds is first prayer, not action; mission is sourced in prayer. The verb ekballō ('cast out') is the same word used for exorcisms — workers are thrust out with urgency.
literary_context: Matt 9:35-38 — Jesus sees the crowds as harassed and helpless; the response commanded is not action but prayer for more workers.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Pray to the Lord of the harvest to send out workers into his harvest.

category: A10 — Include the marginalized / seat the outcast
interpretive_notes: Social hierarchy overturned by gospel identity; 'in the Lord' redefines the relationship; Paul's personal appeal makes it a moral command.
literary_context: Letter to Philemon; Paul appeals for Onesimus, a runaway slave, to be welcomed back as a brother, not a slave.
speaker: Paul
summary: Welcome Onesimus no longer as a slave but as a dear brother — receive him as you would receive me.

category: B8 — Guard sound doctrine / contend for the faith
gk_keywords: politeuesthe (conduct yourselves as citizens); stēkō (stand firm); synathleō (strive together); pistis tou euangeliou
interpretive_notes: synathleō ('striving side by side') is an athletic-competition metaphor; guarding the gospel is a communal, not merely individual, responsibility.
literary_context: Phil 1:27-30 — the central exhortation of the letter's first section; civic-virtue language (politeuesthe — live as citizens) applied to gospel community.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Live worthily of the gospel, standing firm in one spirit and striving together for the faith.

category: A12 — Servant leadership / serve rather than rule
interpretive_notes: Counter-cultural social inversion grounded in Christ's self-emptying (v.6-8); other-focus as the core posture.
literary_context: Christ's humility as the model (Phil 2:1–11); Paul grounds the call to unity and other-focus in the incarnation.
speaker: Paul
summary: In humility count others more significant than yourself — look to the interests of others.

category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
gk_keywords: katergazomai (work out); sōtēria (salvation); phobos kai tromos (fear and trembling); energeō (work in)
interpretive_notes: Holds together human responsibility and divine sovereignty in sanctification. Not about earning salvation but about actively living out what God has already begun.
literary_context: Philippians 2; immediately follows the Christ-hymn (2:5-11); Paul draws the ethical implication of Christ's self-emptying.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Work out your own salvation with fear and trembling — for it is God who works in you, both to will and to work for his good pleasure.

gk_keywords: phroneō (think/have this mind); en Christō Iēsou (in Christ Jesus)
interpretive_notes: Sets up the Christ-hymn as the paradigm for Christian community life. The "mind" (phronēsis) is not mere intellectual agreement but an orientation of will and disposition.
literary_context: Philippians 2; introduces the Christ-hymn (2:6-11); follows the call to unity and humility (2:1-4).
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Have this mind among yourselves, which is yours in Christ Jesus.

interpretive_notes: Eschatological universal confession; grounded in the kenosis of the incarnation; confession is the cosmos's proper response to Christ.
literary_context: Christ's humiliation and exaltation (Phil 2:5–11); the great christological poem; the eschatological goal is universal confession.
speaker: Paul
summary: Every tongue will confess that Jesus Christ is Lord — to the glory of God the Father.

category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
interpretive_notes: Athletic metaphor for Christian perseverance. Paul models what he teaches — the first person gives it authenticity. "Forgetting" is not amnesia but refusing to rest on past achievement.
literary_context: Philippians 3; Paul's autobiographical renunciation of his credentials; pressing toward the goal.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Forgetting what lies behind and straining forward to what lies ahead, I press on toward the goal for the prize of the upward call of God in Christ Jesus.

gk_keywords: alēthēs (true); semnos (honorable); dikaios (just); hagnos (pure); prosphilēs (lovely); logizomai (think/reckon)
interpretive_notes: The most explicit NT command about the content of thought; the list blends Greco-Roman virtue language with Jewish piety. Moved here from D5 as a sanctification/mind command.
literary_context: Phil 4:4-9 — the closing ethical summary; the thought-life command follows the anxiety command and precedes the promise of the God of peace.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Fix your thinking on whatever is true, noble, just, pure, lovely, commendable, excellent, and praiseworthy.

interpretive_notes: Prayer as the structural replacement for anxiety; thanksgiving qualifies the posture of petition.
literary_context: Peace of God (Phil 4:4–9); Paul commands prayer as the antidote to anxiety.
speaker: Paul
summary: Do not be anxious — in everything let your requests be made known to God by prayer with thanksgiving.

interpretive_notes: The command is accompanied by a promise (peace of God). 'Do not be anxious' recurs in Matt 6 as a dominical command; here it is grounded in prayerful trust.
literary_context: Phil 4:4-9 — closing ethical summary; the anxiety command follows 'rejoice in the Lord always' and precedes 'think about these things'.
q2_notes: Creation-level relationship with God; prayer is universal; application is straightforward and consistent across traditions.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Do not be anxious; instead bring everything to God in prayer with thanksgiving.

category: B6 — Keep commandments / obey God's moral law
interpretive_notes: Commandment-keeping and testimony are the two defining marks of the persecuted church in Revelation; obedience is a combat posture.
literary_context: The woman and the dragon (Rev 12); the devil persecutes the church; believers are defined by two marks.
speaker: Narrator
summary: The dragon makes war on those who keep God's commandments and hold Jesus' testimony.

category: A12 — Servant leadership / serve rather than rule
interpretive_notes: Love and service named first among kingdom credentials; increasing works of mercy are commended.
literary_context: Letter to Thyatira (Rev 2:18–29); Christ commends the church; love and service are listed first among the evidences of faithful life.
speaker: Christ / Angel
subtheme: Love and Service as Kingdom Credentials
summary: I know your love, faith, service, and endurance — your latter works exceed the first.

interpretive_notes: Repentance is required even of the most doctrinally faithful church; the call to repent appears in five of the seven letters.
literary_context: Letter to Ephesus (Rev 2:1–7); Christ rebukes the church for abandoning its first love; repentance is the commanded response.
speaker: Christ / Angel
summary: Remember, repent, and return to your first works — or the lampstand will be removed.

category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
interpretive_notes: Faithfulness to the point of death as the ultimate expression of endurance; the reward is life itself.
literary_context: Letter to Smyrna (Rev 2:8–11); Christ addresses a persecuted church; tribulation is imminent; faithfulness unto death is commanded.
speaker: The risen Christ (via John)
summary: Be faithful unto death — I will give you the crown of life.

interpretive_notes: The ultimatum structure (repent or else) appears across the seven letters; the threat is corporate not merely individual, targeting the tolerated false teachers.
literary_context: Rev 2:12-17 — letter to Pergamum; the church is rebuked for tolerating those who hold to the teaching of Balaam and the Nicolaitans.
speaker: The risen Christ (via John)
summary: Repent now — otherwise Christ will come and war against those who hold to false teaching.

interpretive_notes: One of the most concise and urgent commands in the NT; the brevity of the command matches the severity of the situation.
literary_context: Rev 2:8-11 — letter to the church in Smyrna; one of only two letters without rebuke.
speaker: The risen Christ (via John)
summary: Do not fear coming suffering; be faithful even unto death; the crown of life awaits.

interpretive_notes: Self-sufficient wealth produces spiritual blindness; the language of poverty, blindness, and nakedness echoes Jesus' mercy categories ironically.
literary_context: Letter to Laodicea (Rev 3:14–22); Christ rebukes the church for self-sufficient wealth and calls them to recognize their true poverty.
speaker: Christ / Angel
summary: You say 'I am rich and need nothing' — but you are wretched, poor, blind, and naked.

interpretive_notes: Three imperatives (remember, keep, repent) address the progressive spiritual decline; the thief-coming image echoes the parousia warnings in the Synoptics.
literary_context: Rev 3:1-6 — letter to Sardis; a largely dead church is called back to remembrance, keeping, and repentance.
speaker: The risen Christ (via John)
summary: Remember what you received, keep it, and repent — or Christ comes like a thief.

interpretive_notes: The pairing of love with rebuke and discipline draws on Prov 3:12 / Heb 12:6; repentance is not optional but the loving response to divine correction.
literary_context: Rev 3:14-22 — letter to Laodicea; the most severe rebuke of the seven letters; the lukewarm church is called to repentance with urgency.
speaker: The risen Christ (via John)
summary: Be zealous and repent — Christ disciplines those he loves.

gk_keywords: grēgoreō (wake up/be watchful); stērizō (strengthen); mello (about to); apothnēskō (die)
interpretive_notes: Urgent resuscitation command to a dying church. The present tense implies ongoing vigilance. The works are "incomplete" (not absent) — partial obedience is still deficient.
literary_context: Revelation 2-3; letter to Sardis — the "dead" church; between the faithful remnant acknowledgement and the repentance demand.
q2_notes: Addressed to the specific church at Sardis, but the principle of spiritual vigilance is universally applied and consistently taught. No tension with mercy commands.
speaker: The risen Christ (via John)
summary: Wake up, and strengthen what remains and is about to die — for I have not found your works complete in the sight of my God.

interpretive_notes: The logic of mission: salvation requires faith, faith requires hearing, hearing requires preaching — preaching is indispensable.
literary_context: Israel and the gospel (Rom 10:14–17); Paul argues that hearing the gospel requires proclaimers; evangelism is the logical necessity.
speaker: Paul
summary: How shall they believe without hearing? How shall they hear without someone preaching?

interpretive_notes: Mouth and heart together — public confession and internal faith are the twin acts of salvation.
literary_context: The word of faith (Rom 10:5–13); Paul describes the simple, accessible formula of salvation.
speaker: Paul
summary: Confess that Jesus is Lord and believe in his resurrection — you will be saved.

category: A8 — Welcome the stranger / show hospitality
interpretive_notes: Two imperatives: meeting needs of the community and welcoming strangers; philoxenia literally means love of the stranger.
literary_context: Christian ethics in community (Rom 12:9–21); a dense series of imperatives for community life.
speaker: Paul
summary: Contribute to the needs of the saints and seek to show hospitality.

category: A3 — Give to the needy / almsgiving
interpretive_notes: Generosity is a charism, not merely a duty; mercy is named alongside prophecy and teaching as a spiritual gift.
literary_context: Body of Christ and gifts (Rom 12:3–8); Paul lists spiritual gifts; generosity and mercy are included as charisms.
speaker: Paul
summary: Giving with generosity and mercy with cheerfulness are listed as spiritual gifts.

interpretive_notes: Enemy-love expressed as literal food provision; grounds love of enemy in practical, bodily mercy.
literary_context: Christian ethics toward enemies (Rom 12:17–21); Paul quotes Proverbs to ground enemy-love in practical mercy.
speaker: Paul
summary: If your enemy is hungry, feed him; if thirsty, give drink — overcome evil with good.

category: A10 — Include the marginalized / seat the outcast
interpretive_notes: Social equality as gospel ethic; haughtiness and status-seeking are explicitly forbidden.
literary_context: Christian ethics in community (Rom 12:9–21); commands for communal social life.
speaker: Paul
summary: Do not be haughty — associate with the lowly; do not think yourself wise.

gk_keywords: paristēmi (present); thysian zōsan (living sacrifice); logikē latreia (reasonable/spiritual worship)
interpretive_notes: Perhaps the single most important Pauline ethical imperative; the entire body — not just the soul — is the site of worship. "Living sacrifice" redefines sacrifice as embodied daily obedience.
literary_context: Romans 12 opens the paraenetic section; "therefore" connects to the mercies of God expounded in chs. 1-11.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Present your bodies as a living sacrifice, holy and acceptable to God — this is your spiritual worship.

gk_keywords: syschematizō (conform); metamorphoō (transform); anakainōsis (renewal); nous (mind); thelēma Theou
interpretive_notes: The passive imperative metamorphousthe signals that transformation is divine work requiring human cooperation; the nous is the site of ethical discernment. Moved here from D5 as a sanctification command.
literary_context: Rom 12:1-2 — the hinge of Romans from theology to ethics; the foundational transformation command that governs chs.12-15.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Do not conform to this age — be transformed by the renewal of your mind to discern God's will.

interpretive_notes: Paul's version of the great commandment; parallel to Jesus in the Gospels; love is the fulfillment, not just a commandment.
literary_context: Christians and the law (Rom 13:8–10); Paul argues love of neighbor is the fulfillment of the entire law.
speaker: Paul
summary: All commandments are summed up in: love your neighbor as yourself.

interpretive_notes: The theological grounding (authorities from God) qualifies but does not negate the command; does not require obedience to unjust commands (cf. Acts 5:29). The specific mention of taxes (phoros) makes this practically concrete.
literary_context: Rom 13:1-7 — civic duty in the context of the Christian's transformed life (12:1-2); submission to government is part of living under God's order.
q2_notes: The command to submit to governing authorities is universally cited but heavily qualified — evangelicals affirm civil respect in principle but resist when authority contradicts conscience. The tax obligation (v.7) is followed; the unqualified submission (v.1) is not.
q2_typical_application: SELECTIVELY APPLIED (submission affirmed in principle; heavily qualified in practice)
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Every person must be subject to governing authorities — they are instituted by God; pay taxes and give honor due.

interpretive_notes: The principle here is broader than just food — 'or do anything' extends the logic to all exercises of freedom that damage a weaker conscience.
literary_context: Rom 14:13-23 — the strong must not cause the weak to stumble; the governing principle is that personal freedom must yield to brotherly love.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: It is good to refrain from meat or wine or anything that causes your brother to stumble.

audience: The Roman church (strong and weak believers)
interpretive_notes: Those with more freedom must protect those with weaker conscience; the specific dietary context (Jewish vs. Gentile food practices) is broader than idol meat.
literary_context: Rom 14:1-15:7 — extended treatment of the 'strong' and 'weak' on matters of diet and days; probably relates to Jewish food laws and festival observance.
q2_notes: The stumbling-block principle is universally affirmed. Applied broadly beyond dietary disputes to any area of Christian liberty.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Stop judging one another; instead, resolve never to be a stumbling block.

category: A2 — Feed the hungry / provide for bodily needs
interpretive_notes: The collection is framed as a spiritual obligation; Gentile churches owe material support to the Jerusalem church.
literary_context: Paul's travel plans (Rom 15:22–33); Paul describes his upcoming journey and the Jerusalem collection as part of his apostolic mission.
speaker: Paul
summary: Paul carries financial aid to the poor among the saints in Jerusalem — an apostolic mercy mission.

category: A8 — Welcome the stranger / show hospitality
interpretive_notes: proslambanō used of God in Rom 14:3 and here; the welcome of the other person is grounded in and modeled on divine welcome.
literary_context: Rom 15:1-13 — climax of the strong/weak argument; mutual welcome is grounded in Christ's welcome of both groups.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Welcome one another just as Christ welcomed you — for the glory of God.

interpretive_notes: Repeated five times across the NT epistles. Most modern traditions substitute a handshake or embrace; some Orthodox and Coptic communities retain a liturgical kiss practice.
literary_context: Rom 16:1-23 — closing greetings and instructions; one of five NT occurrences of the holy-kiss command.
q2_notes: One of five NT holy-kiss commands. The explicit form (kiss) is universally replaced with a handshake or embrace — a Purpose-Form transfer that is applied here but inconsistently acknowledged elsewhere.
q2_typical_application: IGNORED (form universally replaced; greeting principle retained)
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Greet one another with a holy kiss.

interpretive_notes: The description ('smooth talk, flattery, deceiving the naive') suggests charismatic false teachers; the command is to avoid, not engage or debate.
literary_context: Rom 16:17-20 — Paul's penultimate warning before final greetings.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Watch out for and avoid those who cause divisions and teach contrary to received doctrine.

category: B2 — Believe / have faith in Christ
interpretive_notes: Foundational Pauline statement; faith is the instrument of justification; 'for all' removes ethnic limitation.
literary_context: Righteousness through faith (Rom 3:21–26); Paul's most concentrated statement of justification theology.
speaker: Paul
summary: The righteousness of God comes through faith in Jesus Christ for all who believe.

category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
interpretive_notes: Endurance is not merely tolerated but a productive spiritual virtue; its telos is hope.
literary_context: Peace with God (Rom 5:1–11); Paul describes the progressive fruit of tribulation in the justified life.
speaker: Paul
summary: Suffering produces endurance, endurance character, character hope.

interpretive_notes: Baptism is theologically grounded in union with Christ; the ethical demand (walk in newness) follows from the baptismal identity.
literary_context: Dead to sin, alive in Christ (Rom 6:1–14); Paul grounds ethical transformation in baptismal union with Christ's death and resurrection.
speaker: Paul
summary: We are baptized into Christ's death and raised to walk in newness of life.

interpretive_notes: Three imperatives: reckon, do not let reign, present. The first Pauline ethical command in Romans; flows directly from the indicative of union with Christ in baptism (6:3-4).
literary_context: Romans 6; Paul answers the objection "shall we sin so grace may abound?" — theological basis for sanctification after justification (ch. 5).
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
subtheme: Dead to Sin, Alive to God
summary: Consider yourselves dead to sin and alive to God in Christ Jesus. Do not let sin reign in your mortal body — present your members to God as instruments of righteousness.

gk_keywords: thanatoō (put to death); praxeis tou sōmatos (deeds of the body); pneuma (Spirit)
interpretive_notes: The Spirit-empowered mortification command. Not asceticism but Spirit-enabled killing of sinful patterns. Central to Reformed and Wesleyan sanctification theology.
literary_context: Romans 8; life in the Spirit; follows the flesh/Spirit contrast (8:1-11) and precedes adoption as sons (8:14ff).
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: If by the Spirit you put to death the deeds of the body, you will live — for we are debtors not to the flesh.

category: B8 — Guard sound doctrine / contend for the faith
interpretive_notes: Sound doctrine is a leadership requirement; the elder must both teach it and defend it.
literary_context: Qualifications for elders (Titus 1:5–9); Paul lists requirements for church leadership.
speaker: Paul
subtheme: Elder Must Hold to Sound Doctrine
summary: An elder must hold firm to trustworthy teaching — to instruct and to rebuke contradiction.

gk_keywords: presbyteros; episkopos; anekletos; antechomai; hygiainousa didaskalia
interpretive_notes: The addition of 'so that he may be able to rebuke those who contradict' locates doctrinal fidelity as the reason for the teaching requirement.
literary_context: Titus 1 — Paul's instructions to Titus about appointing elders; parallel to 1 Tim 3 but in a different church situation.
q2_notes: Similar to 1 Tim 3. The command to appoint elders is affirmed, but qualifications are applied unevenly, and "in every town" is not literally followed.
q2_typical_application: BINDING (selective emphasis; "in every town" not applied literally)
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Elders to be appointed in every town; list of character qualifications culminating in holding firmly to sound doctrine.

gk_keywords: sōphroneō (be self-controlled); typos kalōn ergōn (model of good works); aphthorian (integrity); semnotēs (dignity)
interpretive_notes: Self-control (sōphrosynē) is the single virtue demanded of young men in Titus. The emphasis is on exemplary conduct that silences critics.
literary_context: Titus 2; instructions to various groups; between older women (2:3-5) and slaves (2:9-10).
speaker: Paul (via Titus)
summary: Urge the younger men to be self-controlled, showing yourself a model of good works — with integrity and dignity.

interpretive_notes: Evangelistic framing: slave conduct as testimony to the gospel. One of four Pastoral Epistle household code commands.
literary_context: Titus 2 household-code summary covering older men, older women, younger women, younger men, and slaves.
q2_notes: Grounded in witness ("to adorn the doctrine of God") — same grounding used to argue for women's submission in Titus 2:4-5.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Slaves told to be obedient, honest, not argumentative, in order to adorn gospel teaching.

gk_keywords: philandros (loving husband); philoteknos (loving children); sōphrōn (self-controlled); oikouros (working at home); hupotassō (submissive)
interpretive_notes: Unique in that the command is mediated through female teachers (older women); the domestic virtues list combines relational, moral, and social expectations characteristic of Greco-Roman household ethics recontextualised in the church.
literary_context: Titus 2:1-10 — instructions for different household groups; Titus is told to train older women who in turn are to train younger women in domestic virtues.
q2_notes: CRITICAL CASE: The list includes "working at home" (oikouros). If the submission clause is binding, so is the domestic-work clause. Most complementarian churches enforce the submission element while treating the domestic-work element as cultural — an application of Purpose–Form logic that is selectively withheld from other commands.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter, via Titus)
summary: Train young women to love husband and children, be self-controlled, pure, domestic, kind, and submissive.

category: A11 — Bear one another's burdens / help the weak
interpretive_notes: Good works specifically directed toward urgent needs; 'unfruitful' describes the failure to act mercifully.
literary_context: Final instructions (Titus 3:12–15); Paul closes with a command to prioritize practical mercy.
speaker: Paul
subtheme: Devote to Good Works / Urgent Needs
summary: Our people must learn to devote themselves to good works — to help cases of urgent need.

interpretive_notes: Hairetikon (divisive person — root of 'heretic') refers here to someone who causes factions, not necessarily doctrinal error.
literary_context: Titus 3:9-11 — brief instruction on handling divisive people; follows warnings about controversies and genealogies.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: A divisive person should be warned once, then twice, then shunned.

gk_keywords: hypomimnēskō (remind); archais, exousiais (rulers, authorities); peitharchein (obey); hetoimous (ready)
interpretive_notes: The imperative is to remind (hypomimnēskō), not to first instruct — submission to authority is assumed to have been taught; Titus's role is reiteration.
literary_context: Titus 3:1-2 — Titus is instructed to remind believers of their civic duties; the list moves from civic submission to social graciousness.
speaker: Paul (via Titus)
summary: Remind believers to submit to rulers and authorities, to be obedient, ready for every good work.
